ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tofu food product class (FOODON_03311737); no specific agedashi tofu term exists. Closest parent class used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers tofu and tofu products broadly; no agedashi-specific code exists. Closest parent category used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for agedashi tofu or fried tofu dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of agedashi tofu served in a Japanese restaurant typically weighs approximately 250 g, comprising two to four pieces of fried tofu in dashi broth with garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Agedashi Tofu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery-platform source; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (NDB 16427) and retained where consistent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB or USDA for this specific preparation are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — soy and tofu products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for agedashi tofu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to plain fried tofu and silken tofu entries (GI range 15–18) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.93 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 3.3; however, the input record carries a pre-computed GL of 1.3 which corresponds to a 100 g reference portion (15 × 8.93 / 100 ≈ 1.34), consistent with the HPB per-100 g basis. GL per serving retained as 1.3 on the per-100 g basis consistent with source record. Confidence 0.462 reflects analogous estimation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 489 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and dashi broth, with negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid agedashi tofu due to its high sodium content (489 mg/100 g); if consumed, request reduced soy sauce in the dashi broth and limit portion to no more than 100 g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~15), moderate carbohydrate (8.93 g/100 g), low sugar (2.09 g/100 g), and soy protein slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Agedashi tofu is suitable for people with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions; its low glycaemic index and soy protein content minimise postprandial glucose excursions, though the deep-fried preparation adds fat calories that should be accounted for in total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (489 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation contributing saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) and total fat (4.96 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume agedashi tofu sparingly due to its high sodium content and deep-fried preparation; choosing steamed tofu in low-sodium dashi broth is a more heart-healthy alternative, and portion should not exceed 100 g per sitting.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 489 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Agedashi tofu is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation from deep-frying and high sodium; request steamed or silken tofu in dashi broth as an RA-appropriate alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Agedashi tofu (揚げ出し豆腐) is a classic Japanese appetiser consisting of silken or soft tofu that is lightly coated in potato starch (katakuriko) and deep-fried until the exterior forms a delicate, lightly crisp golden crust while the interior remains soft and custardy. The fried tofu pieces are served immediately in a warm, savoury tsuyu broth made from dashi (typically bonito-based), soy sauce, and mirin, which partially softens the starch coating to create a silky, slightly gelatinous texture. The dish is traditionally garnished with finely grated daikon radish, thinly sliced spring onion, and dried bonito flakes (katsuobushi) that dance in the heat of the broth. Nutritionally, agedashi tofu provides 92 kcal per 100 g, with 2.9 g protein from soy, 4.96 g fat from deep-frying, and 8.93 g carbohydrate largely from the starch coating and mirin. Sodium is notably high at 489 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and dashi, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Its low glycaemic index (~15) and soy isoflavone content make it suitable for type 2 diabetes in moderate portions. The dish is widely served in Japanese restaurants in Singapore and is popular as a starter or side dish. It is not vegan due to bonito flakes and dashi, and contains soy and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database at 0.95 confidence. GI estimated by analogy to approved University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried tofu. Micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Japanese local name confirmed from standard culinary references.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for agedashi tofu as a composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/USDA; tofu is a plant-derived product and bonito flakes contribute negligible cholesterol at this serving level). No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (2.9), fat_g (4.96), and carbohydrate_g (8.93) are all non-zero. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on fried tofu entries in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct agedashi tofu GI measurement is available. Calcium (60 mg) and iron (0.8 mg) values from input record originally attributed to a delivery platform have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central fried tofu data and are consistent; retained at reduced confidence. The dish contains bonito flakes (fish) and is therefore not vegetarian or vegan despite the tofu base; dietary_flags updated accordingly.
